nameMeanings.name: Seeley
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: nickname for a person with a cheerful disposition, from Middle English seli ‘happy, fortunate’ (Old English sǣlig, from sǣl ‘happiness, good fortune’). The word was also occasionally used as a female personal name during the Middle Ages. The sense ‘pitiable’, which developed into modern English silly, is not attested before the 15th century. See also Selman .
Altered form of German   Seele  , respelled to preserve the bisyllabic pronunciation of the German name.

variants.name: Selley
meanings: habitational name from Selly Oak in Northfield (Worcestershire), probably from Old English scelf ‘shelf, ledge’ + lēah ‘woodland clearing’.

variants.name: Selman
meanings: English: nickname for a happy or fortunate man, from Middle English seli ‘happy, fortunate’ (Old English sǣlig) + man. The nickname was also used as a personal name. See also Seeley .
English: from the Middle English nickname or female personal name Sely (see Seeley ) + -man, hence an occupational name for a servant of someone so called.

variants.name: Sealy
meanings: Welsh: from the male personal name Selyf or Seleu, medieval Welsh vernacular forms of Solomon which were replaced by that form in the Welsh Bible of 1588.
Irish: variant of   Shelley   (in counties Cork and Kerry); elsewhere of English or Welsh origin, as in 1 above and 2.

variants.name: Seley
meanings: English: variant of   Seeley  . This form of the surname is now rare in Britain.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

en.lifeSummary: When William Seeley was born about 1465, in Great Gransden, Huntingdonshire, England, his father, Isaac Seeley, was 19 and his mother, Amy Elizabeth Slapton, was 13. He had at least 4 sons with Elizabeth. He died on 8 December 1565, in his hometown, at the age of 101, and was buried in Great Gransden, Huntingdonshire, England.

ko.lifeSummary: William Seeley(이)가 대략 1465년 영국 헌틴그도실르 그리트 그랜즈든에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Isaac Seeley(은)는 19세였고, 그의 어머니 Amy Elizabeth Slapton(은)는 13세였다. 그는 Elizabeth(와)과 적어도 네 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1565년 12월 8일 그의 고향에서 101세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 헌틴그도실르 그리트 그랜즈든에 묻혔다.
